title: 8.7 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 8.7!

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did requiring Jewish people to wear armbands (often the yellow Star of David) represent an important early step in their persecution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It publicly branded them as outsiders and enabled their systematic discrimination.
Armbands were used to show which members of the population were protected from persecution.
The use of the Jewish star allowed the Jewish population to express their identity.
It identified which members of the Jewish population were willing to assimilate into German culture.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did Kristallnacht, also known as “the night of broken glass,” reflect the escalation of anti-Jewish policies in Nazi Germany?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It marked the shift from legal discrimination to state-sponsored violence against Germany’s Jewish population.
It was a spontaneous uprising by German citizens, illustrating the growing antisemitism in the public.
It was met with sharp criticism by the international community, increasing calls to intervene.
The German government quickly condemned the actions to distance itself from the perpetrators.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Nazis prioritize women, children, and pregnant individuals for immediate execution upon arrival at death camps?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To prevent the birth of future Jewish generations.
They were viewed as economic burdens.
They lacked the ability to be “re-educated.”
They were most likely to lead a resistance effort.

assessment_items.question_content:
The chart below was created by Nazi Germany as part of the “Nuremberg Laws” to aid in racial identification. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The first column shows people with four non-Jewish grandparents, called “pure German blood” (white circles). People with three or four Jewish grandparents (black circles), shown in the last two columns, were labeled as Jews. Those with one or two Jewish grandparents were considered “mixed race.” 

**In what ways did this racial classification chart support the goals of the Nuremberg Laws?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A German-language chart from 1935 showing how the Nuremberg Laws defined who was considered Jewish based on ancestry. Circles represent family trees with black and white symbols marking Jewish and non-Jewish grandparents, illustrating how the Nazis categorized people as “German,” “mixed,” or “Jewish.”
options.caption: “Nuremberg Laws Chart,” 1935. German Federal Archives (Bundesarchiv), via United States Holocaust Memorial Museum.

choices.content: It provided a legal standard for Nazi’s to systematically identify, classify, and discriminate against the Jewish population.
It gave the Germany military background information to decide who could serve during World War II.
It helped determine who would be held responsible and punished for the atrocities committed during the Holocaust.
It was used as an ancestry tool to help locate families of victims killed in Nazi death camps.

assessment_items.question_content:
Below is a German propaganda poster distributed in German-occupied Poland in 1942. It reads: “Jews Are Lice: They Cause Typhus.”

[[☃ image 1]]

**What was the goal of this type of propaganda?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The poster shows a large, typhus-infected louse crawling across the face of a Jewish man, whose features are distorted to resemble a skull.
options.caption: German antisemitic propaganda poster. Archives of the Institute of National Remembrance, Poland. Public domain.

choices.content: To create fear and justify discrimination and violence against Jews.
To warn people about the actual spread of disease in Europe.
To encourage people to seek medical treatment for typhus.
To promote unity among Jewish and non-Jewish Germans.

assessment_items.question_content:
Poem by Pastor Martin Niemöller (1892–1984), a German clergyman who spoke out after witnessing the dangers of silence during the Nazi era.

>“They came first for the Communists,   and I didn’t speak up because I wasn’t a Communist.  
Then they came for the Jews,   and I didn’t speak up because I wasn’t a Jew.  
Then they came for the trade unionists, and I didn’t speak up because I wasn’t a trade unionist.  
Then they came for the Catholics, and I didn’t speak up because I was a Protestant.  
Then they came for me—and by that time no one was left to speak up.”

**What does this poem reveal about the impact of bystanders during the Holocaust?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When people ignore the suffering of others, injustice spreads and endangers all.
Speaking out against injustice can put people in danger, so silence is safer.
Only religious leaders had the power to stop Nazi persecution.
The Holocaust could have been prevented if all victims had protested.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Manuel Quezon, President of the Philippines, respond to Jewish refugees fleeing Nazi persecution before World War II?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He allowed thousands of Jewish refugees to find safety in the Philippines.
He closed the Philippines’ borders to avoid political complications.
He supported the U.S. policy of limiting refugee immigration.
He sought to deport Jewish refugees back to Europe.

assessment_items.question_content:
>I have instructed the Philippine government to accept Jewish refugees who are fleeing from Nazi persecution. The Philippines, though not wealthy, will do its part to provide them safety and a new home.

>— Manuel Quezon, “Letter to the American Jewish Joint Distribution Committee,” 1938


**What does this statement reveal about Quezon’s attitude toward Jewish refugees?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He supported offering refuge despite the country’s limited resources.
He believed the Philippines was too poor to help refugees.
He thought refugees should only be accepted by wealthy nations.
He opposed accepting refugees to avoid political risks.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Before I leave this world, I want to leave behind a few lines to you, my loved ones. When this letter reaches you one day, I myself will no longer be there... Our end is drawing near. Just like the innocent, defenseless Jews already executed, we are all condemned to death. There is no way for us to escape this horrible, ghastly death.

>—Personal Letter from Tarnopol Ghetto, 1943

**What does this letter reveal about the experience of Jews in Nazi-controlled areas by 1943?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Jews were aware of their fate and lived in constant fear of being killed.
Many Jews still hoped the Nazis would treat them fairly.
Nazi leaders were starting to allow some Jews to emigrate safely.
Most Jewish families could escape if they cooperated with Nazi officials.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Completely nude, men, women, young girls, children, babies... filed by. A heavy SS man told the poor people, ‘No harm will come to you! You just have to breathe deeply—it’s good disinfection!’ … Most of them knew all, the odor confirmed it!

>—Hermann Graebe Testimony, 1945

**What does this testimony reveal about Nazi tactics during mass killings?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Nazis used deception to keep victims calm and prevent resistance.
Victims were forced to fight back before being executed.
The Nazis provided medical treatment to maintain order.
People volunteered to enter gas chambers believing they’d survive.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The Germans... line us up—thirty to forty people—in ranks of four. They lead us outside the ghetto and herd us down the street... One boy tries to escape and takes a series of bullets... The wounded boy is howling... his brother asks the Germans to put him out of his misery but they just laugh.

>—Diary of Stefania Staszewska, 1944)

**What does this diary entry reveal about Nazi treatment of Jewish people during deportations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Deportations were highly organized but deeply inhumane and violent.
The deportations were conducted with care to avoid unnecessary suffering.
Jews could negotiate to avoid being deported.
The German soldiers felt compassion but had to follow orders.
